critic_raw: The concern I would raise is about the evaluative scope. When precision and harmful-error rate are computed only on tickets that passed the 0.92 confidence threshold, the benchmark omits the majority of incoming tickets. If the true quality issue is that the model abstains on difficult tickets while showing high accuracy on easy ones, the reported metrics would look excellent even if the underlying model is not meaningfully better than a simple heuristic on the hard cases. I would also question whether the 37% coverage figure adequately captures the real-world deployment picture — if certain ticket categories are disproportionately routed to humans, that could indicate systematic model blindspots that are invisible in the reported aggregate coverage metric. The evaluation does not describe whether the harmful-error rate is uniformly distributed across ticket types or concentrated in certain categories. Without this breakdown, the 0.7% aggregate figure may obscure meaningful variation.
defender_raw: The evaluation correctly represents the deployed system. This is a selective-prediction pipeline: the model either accepts a ticket and sends an automated reply, or it declines and routes to a human. The design decision — made before training — was to keep harmful-error rate below 1% and to automate at least 30% of volume. The 0.92 threshold was the operationalization of that system requirement, registered before the model was developed. The untouched test set confirmed both targets were met. The critic's concern about whether the model performs well on abstained tickets is conceptually moot — the abstained tickets are handled by humans by design. Reporting harmful-error rate and coverage together is the appropriate two-part metric for this system type. The aggregate coverage figure is correctly reported at the system level. Category-level breakdowns would be useful additional analysis, but their absence does not invalidate the system-level metric given the pre-specified, pre-registration-based evaluation plan.
adjudication_raw: The critic's second-run concern is narrower than run 1 — focusing on category-level abstention distribution and whether aggregate coverage obscures blindspots. This is a valid consideration for a fairness or completeness audit but does not constitute a methodological flaw in the primary evaluation. The pre-registration directly addresses the threshold-selection concern. The system-level metrics are appropriate for the system-level claim. The defender's position holds.
all_issues_raised: aggregate coverage may obscure category-level abstention variation
harmful-error rate may vary across ticket types — no breakdown reported
concern about whether easy-ticket selection inflates apparent performance
